results.possibleIdentity: Juliette Gordon (born 1934) was a prominent American artist and a leader in the New York feminist art movement during the late 1960s and 1970s. She is best known as a co-founder of Women Artists in Revolution (WAR), an activist group that fought for gender equality in the art world, and for authoring the group's first manifesto in 1969. Gordon was also a member of the Art Workers Coalition (AWC) and participated in the Artists Against the Vietnam War movement. Her artistic practice primarily involved political and feminist photocollages, as well as assemblage, printmaking, and drawing. She operated the Juliette Gordon Gallery on East 73rd Street in New York City starting in 1968. Her work was featured in significant exhibitions of the era, including 'Mod Donn Art: 11 Women Artists' (1970) and the 'Ten Downtown' exhibition series (1972). She was the partner of Sam Weinreb, an engineer and artist who was a member of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Gordon continued her practice for decades, later experimenting with digital art before her career was impacted by a major fire at her loft in 2002.
